data.title: DGRO Is A Wealth Compounding Machine, But Nobody Is Talking About Its Biggest Weakness
data.description:
Dividend growth ETFs have become very popular with income investors in recent years because they provide instant diversification as well as relatively attractive current income and consistent dividend growth. In particular, this income growth and diversification come with a single click of the mouse to buy shares in these diversified ETFs, thereby saving investors a lot of time and effort that would otherwise be required to build a diversified dividend growth portfolio on their own by researching, picking, and buying individual dividend stocks.

One of the most popular dividend growth ETFs is the iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), and there is good reason for this. DGRO is a low-cost fund that is well diversified across numerous holdings, and it has delivered attractive total returns and dividend growth over time, making it a proven wealth compounding machine.

However, there are reasons not to own it as well. Previously, I discussed why dividend investors should not own DGRO, and in particular I noted that DGRO is heavily invested in technology stocks, and given that the market increasingly looks like it is in a bubble with weak forward total returns expected for the tech-dominant S&P 500, I think DGRO is unattractive on a valuation basis.

However, there is an even more compelling reason why investors should not own it right now, and in this article I will detail what that is.

DGRO's Core Strengths

Before getting into that negative reason though, it is worth digging further into the positives of DGRO because on the surface it does look like a really strong fund for dividend growth investing. For starters, it offers a 2% trailing 12-month dividend yield, which is nearly twice the yield currently being offered by the S&P 500. On top of that, it charges a very low 0.08% expense ratio that puts it in the same category as many of the lowest cost funds available today.

Additionally, its 10-year dividend growth track record is impressive, as it has increased its dividend every single year over the past decade and done so at a CAGR of 8.91%. While its growth rate has slowed in recent years, with a 6.87% 5-year dividend CAGR and a 6.68% 3-year dividend CAGR, that still comfortably beats inflation and, when combined with its 2% trailing 12-month dividend yield, delivers high single-digit annualized total returns for such a well-diversified and conservatively positioned ETF.

Additionally, when you look at SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), not only is its starting yield much lower, but its dividend growth rate has also been significantly weaker than DGRO's, with a 3-year dividend CAGR of 5.51%, a 5-year dividend CAGR of 5.01%, and a 10-year dividend CAGR of 5.8%. This means that DGRO offers both a higher current yield and consistently stronger dividend growth than SPY does.

DGRO also employs diversification to reduce risks. It holds over 400 companies and diversifies across financials, technology, healthcare, consumer defensive, and industrial stocks, each with roughly 10%–20% exposure, while also maintaining mid-single-digit exposure to utilities, consumer cyclical, energy, and basic materials. Its top 10 holdings account for only 27.24% of the portfolio, and its largest position, Apple (AAPL), represents just 3.31% of the fund, with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) also at 3.31%, thereby preventing over-concentration in any single company.

DGRO also ensures it has only high-quality holdings through its index methodology by tracking the Morningstar U.S. Dividend Growth Index. As such, its holdings must pay a qualified dividend, have at least five years of consecutive dividend growth, maintain an earnings payout ratio below 75%, and remain outside the top decile of dividend yields. These restrictions help filter out potential yield traps and limit exposure to companies with unsustainable payout ratios, while still emphasizing businesses with a strong commitment to paying and growing their dividends.

The fund also uses dividend dollar weighting rather than pure market capitalization weighting, which increases exposure to stocks that pay larger overall dividend dollars rather than just those with larger market caps. This results in a well diversified portfolio of high quality, sustainable dividend growth companies that avoids the most obvious yield traps while still emphasizing companies with a consistent commitment to paying and growing their dividends.

The results speak for themselves, as DGRO has significantly outperformed other dividend focused ETFs such as the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), and the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 High Dividend ETF (SPYD) over long periods of time.

Where DGRO Falls Short

That said, DGRO still has real flaws. The first is that its 2% yield is simply too low for most investors approaching or already in retirement who want to live primarily off dividend income. In fact, if you're going to live off a 2% dividend yield, you might as well just own SPY, which has outperformed DGRO over time, and employ the popular 4% rule or something similar to it, and then rely on a 4% withdrawal rule or something close to it to pay for your living expenses in retirement.

Instead, many investors want a fund that can generate at least 4% dividends that they can live off of, and then grow those dividends at a pace that beats inflation over time, thereby enabling them to actually raise their retirement standard of living over time, rather than have to liquidate their portfolio and potentially be exposed to sequence of returns risk in retirement. Therefore, it provides a sort of unhappy medium in that the yield is too low for someone looking to live off of dividends, and yet the total returns are not as good as many get from simply buying large, passive-managed, low-cost index funds like SPY or the NASDAQ (QQQ).

Additionally, the dividend growth over the past 12 months has been quite weak at just 0.33%, which further calls into question DGRO's merits as a source of passive income for investors looking for an attractive and growing stream of income that investors can depend on. This is especially pronounced given that inflation remains persistently above the Federal Reserve's 2% target rate.

This issue is largely due to the inherent structure of the fund itself because DGRO intentionally excludes high-yielding stocks from its portfolio, and while this does certainly eliminate a lot of underperforming ticking time bombs, it also ignores a lot of high-quality, high-yielding, proven dividend growers like Realty Income (O), Enbridge (ENB), and Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN), not to mention the K1-issuing MLPs like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). All of these stocks pay mid- to high-single-digit yields, yet have grown their dividends year after year after year, and at a bare minimum, have sustained them through periods of economic weakness while also delivering attractive total returns over time.

Therefore, excluding these higher yielding stocks due to the strict rules of the fund is costing DGRO significant income potential as well as, in some cases, even total return potential. Additionally, DGRO's total return outperformance of its other dividend ETFs is largely simply due to the fact that it has over-weighted tech stocks with companies like AAPL, Microsoft (MSFT), and Broadcom (AVGO) in its top 10 holdings, which have pushed its total return higher despite not contributing very much to its dividend-paying potential.

Given that the broader market and tech stocks in particular sit at very lofty valuations, leading legendary investor Howard Marks to recently conjecture that the S&P 500 could be in store for a lost decade of between negative 2% and 2% annualized real returns, this puts DGRO on fairly risky ground.

Investor Takeaway

DGRO has a lot going for it, including its strong track record of delivering long-term attractive total returns and solid dividend growth, its low expense ratio, its high-quality underlying holdings, and its solid diversification. However, its weak yield and unreliable dividend growth due to the way its underlying index is structured make it a relatively poor choice for those looking to live off of passive income in retirement while also keeping it from being an optimal choice for those looking to maximize long-term total returns.

As a result, we avoid funds like DGRO and instead prefer to invest in individual high-quality, higher-yielding stocks, similar to the ones mentioned in this article, like EPD. We then implement an opportunistic capital recycling program that we overlay on top of our value and qualitative-based selection of these high-yielding stocks in order to accelerate the compounding process and deliver long-term total return out performance while simultaneously targeting a 7% weighted average yield and high single-digit to low-teens percentage annualized dividend growth rate in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: CURE: Analysts Strike A Cautious Tone Heading Into 2026
data.description:
Introduction

Amid recent worries about the valuation of AI stocks, defensive sectors such as health care have seen an uptick in investor attention. Indeed the unlevered Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLV) is marginally outperforming the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) so far in 2025, while the leveraged Direxion Daily Healthcare Bull 3X Shares ETF (CURE) stands out with its 32% year-to-date gain:

I have covered CURE several times, most recently highlighting the attractive valuation of its underlying index relative to the S&P 500 in August 2025. Given the significant move in health care stocks in recent months, now is the perfect time to reevaluate CURE's potential going into 2026.

As you may expect, health care stocks are not as cheap as they were, trading at about 20.1x their forward earnings. Even so, I confirm my Buy rating on CURE, driven by:

  • A significant 19% discount relative to the S&P 500 based on forward earnings, providing a sizable margin of safety even if earnings growth falls short of expectations.
  • Increased demand for safe health care stocks amid U.S. economic uncertainty/ongoing Fed rate cuts.
  • Favorable long-term trends such as ageing and an increased focus on health and wellbeing which are driving demand for health care products.

In this article, I will provide an update on analyst expectations for the next 12 months, as well as discuss risks to the investment case.

ETF Overview

You can access all CURE details on the Direxion website here. The Direxion Daily Healthcare Bull 3X Shares ETF seeks to deliver 300% (3x) of the daily return of the Health Care Select Sector Index, an index focused on large-cap U.S. healthcare stocks.

Investors in CURE may also find useful and more up-to-date information by looking at the State Street website for the unleveraged XLV ETF which tracks the same underlying index. There we find that as of late November 2025, the underlying index is quite concentrated, with the top ten positions accounting for ~58.17% of all assets:

Relative to my prior coverage, index concentration has marginally increased, principally driven by a higher weight of Eli Lilly (LLY).

CURE's expense ratio is quite elevated, at 0.93%, but largely in line with other 300% leveraged ETFs. Given the high expense ratio, you should view CURE as more of a trading tool, notwithstanding the long-term potential growth rate of healthcare stocks (which I discussed in my first CURE article here).

Indeed when discussing ETFs with friends, I always advise them to pick ETFs with fees of 0.1%-0.2% for long-term passive holdings. In this respect, the unlevered XLV ETF, with an expense ratio of only 0.08%, ticks all the boxes.

Valuation remains attractive

I calculate that the Health Care Select Sector Index trades at a forward P/E of 20.1x, notably higher than the 17.3x multiple observed in August this year. Even so, the U.S. health care sector remains cheaper than the S&P 500, which trades at roughly 24.75x its forward earnings. As such, I believe that from a valuation perspective, investors are getting a decent margin of safety even if earnings growth at health care companies falls short of expectations.

Indeed, if we look at current valuations, the health care sector is notably more expensive, with a trailing P/E of 27.4x, broadly in line with the overall S&P 500.

Analysts Strike A Cautious Tone

In my prior coverage of CURE I highlighted that 12-month price targets of Wall Street analysts envisaged a weighted average gain of 12.05% for the underlying index's largest holdings.

Fast forward to November 2025, we observe that recent price gains have substantially diminished upside potential to about 0.77% over the next 12 months, as shown in the table below:

Company Weight 12-month Upside Potential
Eli Lilly (LLY) 15.12% -7.74%
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) 8.82% -2.42%
AbbVie (ABBV) 7.20% +5.07%
UnitedHealth (UNH) 5.13% +18.52%
Merck (MRK) 4.46% -1.32%
Abbott Laboratories (ABT) 3.93% +12.79%
Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) 3.93% +2.64%
Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) 3.62% +2.43%
Amgen (AMGN) 3.20% -6.09%
Gilead Sciences (GILD) 2.76% +2.76%
Total 58.17% 0.77%

Source: Author's calculations

The silver lining from the table above is that the drag on returns is principally concentrated in the index's largest holding, Eli Lilly. Hence I would estimate that the remaining 41.83% of ETF assets would, on average, deliver higher returns over the next 12 months. As a result, I expect CURE's underlying index to outperform the 0.77% average analyst price gain over the next 12 months.

CURE Rating

On the one hand, we observe that forward earnings estimates for CURE's underlying index point to significant earnings growth over the next year, resulting in a still attractive 20.1x forward earnings multiple (at least relative to the S&P 500).

On the other hand, the bull case is clearly not as strong as it was a few months ago, with analysts projecting modest single-digit price gains for the underlying index's largest holdings.

Against this backdrop, also taking into account ongoing Fed policy normalization (which naturally increases demand for safer investments such as health care stocks), I remain cautiously optimistic for CURE over the next 12 months. Indeed the ETF will likely not deliver the outstanding returns it has achieved in 2025. At the same time, I am more cautious toward overall market returns, making defensive allocation valuable in my opinion.

All in all, weighing the pros and cons, I confirm my Buy rating on CURE, although I do see the rationale of taking profits here and potentially switching to the unlevered XLV ETF.

My bull rating is driven by favorable long-term factors for the health care sector, near-term economic uncertainty in the U.S. economy (which hopefully will diminish going into 2026), and still attractive valuations relative to the overall U.S. stock market.

Risks

While CURE has delivered solid results so far in 2025, 300% leveraged ETFs are prone to significant near-term drawdowns. For instance, the ETF crashed over 60% during the COVID-19 market panic in March 2020, despite the pandemic ultimately being a money maker for drug and vaccine manufacturers. As such, sound long-term fundamentals cannot guarantee robust near-term returns.

Investors in 300% leveraged ETFs should also consider the effect of beta slippage (the tracking error of leveraged ETFs resulting from prices moving up and down). Indeed beta slippage, an elevated expense ratio, and a significant drop of over 30% during the April 2025 market volatility resulted in CURE only delivering a gain of 32% year-to-date. This is below the circa 50% return one would expect from a perfect 300% replication of XLV's year-to-date performance.

Investors who cannot bear the volatility associated with CURE's daily movements but still want to capture the upside offered by health care stocks should consider the unleveraged XLV ETF. You can read more about the risk-return profile and risks associated with leveraged ETFs on the SEC website here.

Last but not least, I would note that significant earnings growth is a key pillar of the CURE investment case. As such, should earnings growth fall short of expectations, both CURE and XLV are likely to underperform the broader market.

Conclusion

After a slow start to 2025, the health care sector is finally back on the radar of investors, with the unlevered XLV ETF marginally outperforming the S&P 500, while the levered CURE has naturally performed much better, delivering gains of about 32%.

Against the backdrop of market euphoria, I still believe that a long CURE position makes sense in 2026. This is principally driven by attractive forward earnings estimates, as well as increased demand for safe health care stocks amid U.S. economic uncertainty and ongoing Fed policy normalization.

While I rank CURE a Buy, I understand the hesitancy some investors may have with regard to the risk/reward potential going into 2026. As such, switching some of the CURE allocation to the unlevered XLV ETF may make sense for conservative investors.

Despite favorable long-term trends, investors in CURE should pay attention to risks stemming from 300% leverage, beta slippage and a high expense ratio, as well as the possibility of future earnings falling short of expectations.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Corporacion America Airports: Staying Bullish After Strong Q3 Earnings
data.description:
Argentine airport operator Corporation America Airports (NYSE:CAAP) released its latest quarterly earnings earlier this week. I've been a long-time shareholder of this company and have been enjoying its ascent over the years:

With CAAP stock at fresh all-time highs, a new concession being arranged, and the latest earnings to review, it's a great time to update my outlook on the company.

CAAP Stock Lifts Off: Here's Why

The last few times I've written about Corporacion America Airports, the shares have been trading around $20. After being stuck around that level all year, however, CAAP stock has now jumped to $24. There are three main factors which have driven the price action since my prior update.

The first of these is the broader macroeconomic backdrop. Corporacion America Airports derives slightly over half its revenues and EBITDA from Argentina.

It's no secret that Argentina is a highly volatile economy. It just recently came out of a hyperinflationary period where the annual inflation rate topped 200%. While Corporacion America Airports gets paid in U.S. Dollars for international passengers arriving in Argentina, the fact that it relies on that economy for the majority of its business has been enough to scare investors historically.

As a result, CAAP shares have generally traded at much lower multiples than other comparable airport operators. The publicly-traded Mexican airports, for example, generally trade at significantly higher EV/EBITDA multiples than Corporacion America Airports even though Mexico has political and economic instability of its own.

In any case, Argentina's politics have swung to the right since 2023. Libertarian pro-business president Javier Milei created a great deal of excitement among investors, and Argentine stocks had been some of the strongest performers of the past few years. Notably, Milei also was the chief economist at Corporacion America in private life prior to becoming president. Not surprisingly, as president, he's helped push deregulation and increased competition within the Argentine airline industry, which has helped power its above-average traffic growth in recent years.

I mention this since Argentina had its midterm legislative elections in October. Analysts had been worried that Milei's party could suffer a setback in those elections. The Argentine Peso had come under pressure earlier this year, and this led to concerns about the sustainability of Milei's reform agenda. Back in September, however, I urged CAAP shareholders to avoid getting shaken out by political uncertainty.

That turned out well. In October, Argentina received U.S. financial backing ahead of that election which calmed down the situation. Milei's party went on to deliver results way ahead of expectations and Argentine stocks skyrocketed on the news. CAAP stock, for its part, jumped more than 15% in the days following the election. While Corporacion America Airports is far from the most highly-correlated bet on Argentina's macroeconomic situation, a rising tide lifts all boats; CAAP stock should benefit as investor sentiment improves.

While CAAP stock jumped on broader Argentine developments, the company's core operations continue firing on all cylinders as well.

Q3 Earnings Impress

We can see that with the company's Q3 results.

Revenues of $527 million rose 14% year-over-year. EPS of 34 cents surged from the nine cents reported in the same period of 2024. Adjusted EBITDA soared 35% higher versus the same quarter in 2024. And the company's EBITDA margin (excluding construction services) leapt more than 500 bps higher to 41.2% year-over-year.

Passenger traffic was up 9% in Q3, led by the strength in Argentina where CAAP once again posted double-digits year-over-year growth. And CAAP was able to transform that 9% rise in traffic growth into a 15% pop in aeronautical revenues and a 13% increase in non-aeronautical revenues.

If you dive into the details, you'll find a business that is benefiting from favorable traffic patterns at several of its key airports. It is also spending on big investments (such as the new shopping mall at the Brasilia airport) which can drive much higher revenues per passenger going forward.

The one bit of caution out of the Q3 report would be this comment from the CEO, Martín Eurnekian:

"Looking ahead, we expect positive traffic momentum to continue, although domestic growth in Argentina is anticipated to moderate from the exceptionally strong levels seen earlier in the year. Overall, we anticipate solid results in the fourth quarter, although not benefiting from the easier comparisons that supported third-quarter robust performance in Argentina."

That more restrained outlook is duly noted. Keep in mind, however, that other airport operators have seen their traffic level off to essentially flat results year-over-year amid a weaker global economy. It would have been a challenge for Argentina to keep up the double-digit traffic growth rate indefinitely given the global macro situation. Regardless, it appears Argentina can keep outperforming the overall global aviation market.

Making Sense of CAAP's Newest Airport

The final important item is the company's new concession. Specifically, Corporacion America Airports has reached an agreement to operate Iraq's flagship Baghdad international airport.

I will be honest, I was not especially enthused when I saw that Corporacion America Airport's next property would be Baghdad. Iraq has had various geopolitical and economic struggles over the years. Indeed, U.S. soldiers were killed in a 2003 fight to seize control of Baghdad's airport. Investors probably don't associate Iraq with a bustling commercial aviation market.

However, let me make the case for why this might be a reasonable venture. Corporacion America Airports' management team has a strategy of not just operating mature airport assets but also taking a build-and-expand approach. The company looks for opportunities where it can find underutilized or inadequate infrastructure and make large investments such as in new terminals, runways, shopping centers, etc. to generate much more tourist traffic and improve revenues per passenger that uses a CAAP facility.

The company offers potential counterparties a large investment in a concessions' airport infrastructure, thus potentially greatly boosting traffic potential at that property and creating new jobs and tax revenues for the local area. In return, CAAP is able to lock-in high guaranteed IRRs on these expenditures. If a contract is not living up to the stipulated IRR, it gets some sort of compensation; for example, see the Argentine contracts, which the government extended from 2028 to 2038 as relief during the pandemic lockdowns.

I haven't seen the exact details of the Iraq contract yet, but based on past experience, it seems likely that CAAP will be able to protect its funds as it expands and modernizes the Baghdad facility. CAAP has cash on hand (more than half a billion dollars as of this latest quarter), and Iraq has a capital city airport that needs significant expansion. This can be a win-win for both sides.

And while Iraq is not at the top of most people's travel wishlists, that doesn't mean the commercial market is tiny. Iraq still has a ton of oil, and there will always be business travel associated with that. In addition, as a national capital, Baghdad is assured of getting plenty of traffic from politicians, diplomats, non-governmental organization workers and so on. Furthermore, I'd note that countries such as Saudi Arabia, U.A.E., and even Kuwait (population: 5 million) have generated robust aviation sectors.

I'm not forecasting that Iraq's airline sector will see that level of success anytime soon, but this could be a much bigger property than investors anticipate. To that point, CAAP is ultimately planning to expand Baghdad's airport to be able to handle 15 million passengers per year. If that capacity comes into use, this would put the airport in a position to be a top five airport within CAAP's overall portfolio.

On the latest conference call, management said that it will give us more concrete information on Baghdad once the final contract is agreed upon and becomes binding. I'll reserve complete judgment until seeing that info. For now, I don't love the deal, but I don't mind it either. CAAP's past M&A has been good, and I'm willing to see how Iraq plays out within the broader portfolio.

CAAP Stock Verdict

Corporacion America Airports continues to add to its business, both with the Iraq deal and several other potential concessions where CAAP is an involved bidder. The firm has a strong balance sheet, with Debt/EBITDA falling below 1.0x recently. I look forward to management deploying its balance sheet at the firm's usual high IRRs to kick off the next leg of the growth story.

In the meantime, core operations are continuing to deliver. The Argentine aviation market, in particular, has been a huge winner in 2025 at a time when so many others have seen significant deceleration. And now, with Milei having a fresh mandate in Argentina, that should pave the way for further deregulation and pro-business measures going forward.

Just look at that Q3 earnings report. The growth story is playing out as expected. And with Argentina giving Milei an electoral mandate in the October vote, that lifts the near-term political overhang that had been holding CAAP shares down. All this sets the stage for this airport operator to continue its move to new highs.

data.title: Alphabet: Why It Beat The Rest Of The Magnificent 7 During The Market Pullback
data.description:
Is This an AI Bubble?

When the market sold off last week on renewed fears of AI bubble and stretched valuations, Alphabet Inc. (GOOG, GOOG:CA, GOOGL) had reached new all-time highs. Over the past four weeks, the stock is up 20% and has been the top performer among the Magnificent 7. So what's behind this bullish momentum?

First, GOOGL's valuation is still pretty reasonable, despite nearly doubling its P/E since the April low. Second, the company delivered a strong 3Q earnings, driven by accelerating AI monetization, and its higher capex has not hurt FCF. Demand for enterprise AI solutions remains robust. I think the recent debut of Gemini 3.0 should only add more momentum. Third, there is a growing speculation on Google chips, which has a potential to challenge Nvidia (NVDA)'s leadership. Therefore, strong fundamentals, reasonable valuation, and bullish sentiment (including positive antitrust outcomes in September) have all contributed to this strong upside momentum.

In my previous analysis, I maintained a Buy on GOOGL because I saw a turnaround opportunity when the stock was lagging its peers and its valuation had contracted below FY2024 levels. The stock had been out of favor as growth slowed. At that time, I pointed out that the market was rewarding AI companies showing strong growth acceleration. GOOGL ended up delivering exactly that in its Q2 and Q3 FY2025 earnings.

Now, the stock is trading at 30x non-GAAP P/E fwd, which still has room for more upside. It may see some near-term consolidation due to its overbought levels. GOOGL has held up extremely well during the recent market selloff. I believe the quality of the stock has improved significantly compared to three months ago. Therefore, GOOGL is still a solid Buy.

Why Google Outperformed META by 35% in One Month

GOOGL's revenue growth started reaccelerating again in Q2 FY2025, driven by advertising revenue (which made up 72.5% of total revenue in the recent quarter). There are many factors that can influence near-term stock sentiment. You might ask: if Meta Platforms' (META) advertising revenue growth accelerated faster than GOOGL's over the last two quarters, why is META's stock down 20% over the past month whereas GOOGL was up 15%?

As I explained in META's recent earnings, a stock's quality largely depends on its bottom-line, such as earnings and FCF. Despite META achieving a stronger top-line growth, its EPS growth slowed to 20% YoY in 3Q FY2025, below GOOGL's 35.4% YoY. Most importantly, GOOGL's FCF over the last 4 quarters (trailing twelve months) grew 32% YoY, while META's FCF instead dropped 15.5% YoY.

Although both companies raised their capex outlooks, I think GOOGL executed far better in cost management while still maintaining a positive FCF growth. From a discounted cash flow standpoint, GOOGL justifies a higher stock quality compared to META.

In addition, META is considering a multi-billion-dollar deal with GOOGL for its TPUs. This news has fueled speculation that GOOGL could challenge NVDA's AI chip leadership, which pushed the stock even higher.

Google Cloud: Scaling AI Monetization and Improving Cost Efficiency

Google Cloud revenue continued accelerating, growing 33.5% YoY in 3Q. However, we also saw both Microsoft's (MSFT) Azure and Amazon's (AMZN) AWS accelerate their revenue growths in the last two quarters, indicating that AI demand remained robust across the hyperscalers. I think what really makes GOOGL stand out is that its elevated capex hasn't hurt its margins, showing strong cost efficiency. Google Cloud's operating margin expanded to 23.7%, up 590 bps compared to 1Q FY2025.

GOOGL does not provide Remaining Performance Obligation (RPO) metrics, which represents total contract backlogs that will be recognized as future revenue. However, management indicated in the earnings call that demand for Enterprise AI infrastructure has been incredibly strong, driven by Gemini 2.5. Cloud backlog reached $155 billion in 3Q, up 46% from the previous quarter. They also noted that the number of +$1 billion deals signed in FY2025 is greater than the total from the past two years combined.

For 4Q FY2025 and FY2026, GOOGL expects a "tight demand-supply environment," especially as the recent release of Gemini 3.0 further boosts AI demand and AI monetization. Given this outlook, I don't see any near-term slowdown for GOOGL.

"Significantly Higher" Capex in FY2026

Even though both META and GOOGL called for significantly higher capex in FY2026, I think GOOGL is in a much better position. First, as mentioned earlier, elevated capex hasn't really pressured its FCF, which is still up 32% YoY on a TTM basis. Second, capex as a percentage of total revenue ratio hasn't increased that much. We can see on the chart that GOOGL's capex has been accelerating this year. In Q3 FY2025, the company raised the F2025 capex outlook to $91 to $93 billion, up from $85 billion guided in Q2. Using the midpoint of $92 billion, we can calculate that its 4Q capex growth would be 99% YoY. The Q4 total revenue consensus is $110.1 billion, which implies the capex ratio would increase to 25.6%.

By contrast, META's capex grew 110.5% YoY in Q3, marking the third consecutive quarter above 100% growth. Moreover, its capex as a percentage of total revenue reached 37.8%, compared to GOOGL's 23.4%. This indicates that META is investing more aggressively in AI infrastructure, raising concerns about potential overspending, as its FCF has suffered from big declines over the past three quarters. More details can be found in my recent META's analysis.

Valuation Is Still Reasonable

On the valuation side, GOOGL is currently overbought as RSI 14 is above 80. This can lead to near-term consolidation. At this level, the stock still looks relatively cheap compared to the broader AI market. GOOGL's GAAP P/E TTM is currently at 31.4x, nearly double from 16.3x in April low. However, the multiple is still cheaper than MSFT's 33.7x and AMZN's 32x. It's also lower than the Nasdaq index's P/E TTM of 32x.

Looking forward, GOOGL trading at 30x non-GAAP P/E fwd still looks reasonable to me, given its scaling AI monetization. Its growth and margin profile is improving. The company still generates FCF, despite maintaining an elevated capex.

This also helps explain why META is trading at a low valuation multiple, at 23.7x non-GAAP P/E fwd, because its aggressive AI investments are pressuring margins and FCF, lowering the stock's quality in the near term.

In addition, GOOGL's multiple expansion can now be lifted by growing optimism around Google chips, which has potential to take market shares from NVDA. META is considering spending billions of dollars on GOOGL's Tensor Processing Unit aka TPUs.

Conclusion

In summary, I don't think GOOGL's 72% rally since my last rating has been largely driven by AI bubble. We see both advertising and cloud revenue have been growing strongly, and improved cost efficiency has significantly pushed margins higher, resulting in stronger earnings growth. Despite higher capex, the company still maintains a positive FCF growth. Google AI chips now have also lifted market sentiment. Even so, trading at 28.5x non-GAAP P/E fwd still looks cheap to me. Therefore, I believe GOOGL remains a high conviction bet: It's a high-quality, AI growth stock with a reasonable valuation, deserving a Buy rating.
